---
title: "Chapter 28 — 💬 Natural Language Processing"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 27 · 👁️ Computer Vision](ch27.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [29 · 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing →](ch29.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Natural Language Processing (NLP) is the branch of AI concerned with teaching machines to read, understand, and generate human language — the messy, ambiguous, context-dependent code we use to communicate. It matters because most of the world's knowledge lives in text, and a system that can process language unlocks search, translation, assistants, and analysis at scale. This chapter walks the classical-to-neural arc: how the field turned words into numbers, the statistical models that dominated for decades, the core tasks every practitioner should know, and how transformers reset the playing field.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Language, Vision & Modalities · turning raw text into structured meaning and fluent output · the central idea is that language must first be represented as numbers, and every advance is a better representation.
:::

## 28.1 — From characters to tokens: text preprocessing

Raw text is a stream of characters, but almost no model works on characters directly. The first job of any NLP pipeline is to chop that stream into meaningful units and normalize away noise that would otherwise multiply the vocabulary needlessly. Think of it as turning a paragraph into a clean list of building blocks before any learning begins.

The opening step is **tokenization**: splitting text into **tokens**, the atomic units a model sees. The naive approach splits on whitespace and punctuation, so `"Dr. Smith isn't home."` becomes `["Dr", ".", "Smith", "is", "n't", "home", "."]`. Notice the decisions hiding here — does `isn't` become one token or two, and is `Dr.` an abbreviation or the end of a sentence? These edge cases are why robust tokenizers are rule-heavy. Modern systems use **subword tokenization** (Byte-Pair Encoding, WordPiece) that splits rare words into reusable pieces so that `unhappiness` becomes `un`, `happi`, `ness` — keeping the vocabulary bounded while handling words never seen in training.

The intuition behind subword tokenization is a balancing act. A pure word vocabulary chokes on anything it has not seen before (every typo, name, or new term becomes a useless "unknown" token), while a pure character vocabulary makes sequences painfully long and forces the model to relearn spelling from scratch. Subword methods strike the middle: keep frequent whole words intact, but break rare words into common fragments the model already understands. Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) builds this vocabulary greedily — start from individual characters, then repeatedly merge the most frequent adjacent pair into a new symbol until you hit a target vocabulary size.

```python
from collections import Counter

# BPE: learn merges by repeatedly fusing the most frequent adjacent pair
def learn_bpe(words, num_merges):
    # each word is a list of chars ending in an end-of-word marker "_"
    corpus = {tuple(list(w) + ["_"]): c for w, c in words.items()}
    merges = []
    for _ in range(num_merges):
        pairs = Counter()
        for sym, freq in corpus.items():
            for a, b in zip(sym, sym[1:]):      # count adjacent pairs
                pairs[(a, b)] += freq
        if not pairs:
            break
        best = max(pairs, key=pairs.get)        # most frequent pair
        merges.append(best)
        # rewrite the corpus, fusing 'best' wherever it occurs
        new = {}
        for sym, freq in corpus.items():
            out, i = [], 0
            while i < len(sym):
                if i < len(sym) - 1 and (sym[i], sym[i+1]) == best:
                    out.append(sym[i] + sym[i+1]); i += 2
                else:
                    out.append(sym[i]); i += 1
            new[tuple(out)] = freq
        corpus = new
    return merges

m = learn_bpe({"low": 5, "lower": 2, "newest": 6, "widest": 3}, num_merges=4)
print(m[0])  # ('e', 's') — the most common adjacent pair gets merged first
assert m[0] == ("e", "s")
```

After tokenizing, classical pipelines normalize. **Stop-word** removal drops ultra-common words (`the`, `is`, `of`) that carry little topic signal. **Stemming** chops words to a crude root with rules — `running`, `runs`, `ran` all map toward `run` (the Porter stemmer would turn `studies` into `studi`, not even a real word). **Lemmatization** does the same job properly, using a dictionary and part-of-speech to map a word to its true base form (**lemma**), so `better` becomes `good` and `studies` becomes `study`. Lemmatization is slower but linguistically correct; stemming is fast and crude.

```python
import re
# minimal whitespace+punctuation tokenizer with a Porter-style suffix strip
def tokenize(text):
    return re.findall(r"[a-z]+|[^\w\s]", text.lower())  # words or punctuation

def crude_stem(tok):
    for suf in ("ing", "ed", "s"):           # order matters: longest first
        if tok.endswith(suf) and len(tok) - len(suf) >= 3:
            return tok[: -len(suf)]
    return tok

toks = tokenize("The runners studied harder, running daily.")
print([crude_stem(t) for t in toks])  # ['the','runner','studi','harder',',','runn','daili','.']
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Aggressive preprocessing can destroy signal. Removing stop-words wrecks sentiment (`not good` becomes `good`), and stemming collapses distinctions a downstream task may need. Modern transformer pipelines skip stemming and stop-word removal almost entirely — the model learns what matters. Reach for these steps only with bag-of-words style classical models.
:::

## 28.2 — Turning words into vectors: count-based representations

A model cannot multiply the word `cat`. It needs numbers, so the central problem of NLP is **representation**: how do we encode text as vectors? The oldest answer is to count.

The **bag-of-words** (BoW) model represents a document as a vector over the vocabulary, where each entry is the count of that word in the document. "Bag" because order is thrown away — `dog bites man` and `man bites dog` get identical vectors. Crude, but for topic-level tasks (is this email about sports or finance?) word presence alone is surprisingly powerful.

Raw counts over-reward common words, so we weight them with **TF-IDF** (term frequency–inverse document frequency). The intuition: a word matters in a document if it appears often *here* but rarely *everywhere else*. Term frequency captures the first part, inverse document frequency the second:

$$\text{tf-idf}(t, d) = \underbrace{\text{tf}(t,d)}_{\text{count in doc}} \times \underbrace{\log\frac{N}{1 + \text{df}(t)}}_{\text{rarity across corpus}}$$

where $N$ is the number of documents and $\text{df}(t)$ is how many documents contain term $t$. A word like `the` appears everywhere, so its IDF is near zero and it gets crushed; a distinctive term like `photosynthesis` survives with high weight.

```python
import numpy as np
# TF-IDF from scratch on a tiny 3-document corpus
docs = [["the","cat","sat"], ["the","dog","sat"], ["the","cat","ran"]]
vocab = sorted({w for d in docs for w in d})          # ['cat','dog','ran','ran'...]
N = len(docs)

def tf(doc):                                          # term frequency per doc
    return np.array([doc.count(w) / len(doc) for w in vocab])

df = np.array([sum(w in d for d in docs) for w in vocab])  # doc frequency
idf = np.log(N / (1 + df))                            # rarity across corpus
tfidf = np.array([tf(d) * idf for d in docs])
# 'the' is in every doc -> idf small -> column near zero
print(vocab[np.argmin(idf)])  # 'the' — crushed for being ubiquitous
```

To claw back a little word order, we use **n-grams** — contiguous spans of $n$ tokens. A bigram model treats `new york` and `machine learning` as single features, capturing local phrasing that unigrams miss. The cost is a vocabulary explosion: bigrams can number in the millions, making vectors enormous and sparse.

| Representation | Captures order? | Vocabulary size | Typical use |
|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------|
| Bag-of-words   | No              | Vocab           | Topic classification |
| TF-IDF         | No              | Vocab           | Search, document similarity |
| N-grams        | Local only      | Explodes with $n$ | Phrase-sensitive features |

The deep limitation of all count vectors is that they are **sparse** and **orthogonal**: `cat` and `kitten` share no dimensions, so the model sees them as unrelated as `cat` and `democracy`. That blind spot motivates the next section.

## 28.3 — Dense meaning: word embeddings

Counting tells you *which* words appear but nothing about what they *mean*. The breakthrough idea, rooted in the **distributional hypothesis** — "you shall know a word by the company it keeps" — is that a word's meaning can be inferred from the contexts it appears in. Words used in similar contexts (`king`, `queen`, `monarch`) should land near each other in space.

A **word embedding** is a dense, low-dimensional vector (say 300 numbers) learned so that this geometric closeness reflects semantic similarity. Unlike a one-hot count vector with one non-zero entry, every dimension here is a meaningful real number, and `cat` and `kitten` end up as near-neighbors.

**word2vec** learns these vectors with a deceptively simple trick: train a shallow network to predict a word from its neighbors (CBOW) or the neighbors from a word (skip-gram). You never care about the prediction — you keep the hidden-layer weights, which become the embeddings. **GloVe** reaches a similar place by factorizing a global word co-occurrence matrix instead of sliding a window. Both yield the famous property that *directions* in the space carry meaning:

$$\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$$

```python
import numpy as np
# cosine similarity: the standard way to compare two embeddings
def cosine(a, b):
    return a @ b / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))

# toy 3-d "embeddings" — real ones are ~300-d and learned from data
king  = np.array([0.9, 0.7, 0.1]); queen = np.array([0.8, 0.9, 0.15])
car   = np.array([0.1, 0.2, 0.95])
print(round(cosine(king, queen), 3))  # high — related royalty terms
print(round(cosine(king, car),   3))  # low  — unrelated
```

Here is the geometry: similar words cluster, and the *offset* between related pairs is roughly constant.

<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <line x1="40" y1="120" x2="290" y2="120" stroke="#888" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="120" x2="40" y2="20" stroke="#888" stroke-width="1"/>
  <circle cx="90" cy="95" r="4" fill="#4477cc"/><text x="98" y="99" font-size="11">man</text>
  <circle cx="150" cy="55" r="4" fill="#4477cc"/><text x="158" y="59" font-size="11">king</text>
  <circle cx="120" cy="100" r="4" fill="#cc4477"/><text x="128" y="104" font-size="11">woman</text>
  <circle cx="180" cy="60" r="4" fill="#cc4477"/><text x="188" y="64" font-size="11">queen</text>
  <line x1="90" y1="95" x2="150" y2="55" stroke="#4477cc" stroke-dasharray="3" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="120" y1="100" x2="180" y2="60" stroke="#cc4477" stroke-dasharray="3" stroke-width="1"/>
</svg>

The key weakness of word2vec and GloVe is that each word gets *one* vector regardless of context, so `bank` (river vs. money) is forced into a single compromised point. Fixing that — **contextual** embeddings — is exactly what transformers brought, covered in 28.6.

::: {.callout-tip}
Cosine similarity, not Euclidean distance, is the default for comparing embeddings. Direction encodes meaning; magnitude often just reflects word frequency, so normalizing it away is usually what you want.
:::

## 28.4 — The sequence-labeling toolkit: HMMs and CRFs

Many NLP tasks are not about whole documents but about labeling each token in order. **Part-of-speech (POS) tagging** assigns `noun`, `verb`, `adjective` to each word; **named-entity recognition (NER)** marks spans as `Person`, `Organization`, `Location`. The defining feature is that labels are interdependent — a determiner is usually followed by a noun, not a verb — so you must model the *sequence*, not each token in isolation.

The classic generative model is the **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)**. It imagines a hidden chain of tags generating the observed words: each tag depends only on the previous tag (the **Markov assumption**), and each word is emitted by its tag. It is defined by transition probabilities $P(\text{tag}_i \mid \text{tag}_{i-1})$ and emission probabilities $P(\text{word}_i \mid \text{tag}_i)$. To find the best tag sequence, the **Viterbi algorithm** uses dynamic programming to search all paths efficiently.

The Viterbi trick is worth seeing concretely. A brute-force search over every possible tag sequence is exponential — with $T$ tags and a sentence of length $n$, there are $T^n$ paths. Viterbi collapses this to $O(n T^2)$ by a simple observation: the best path *through* a given tag at position $i$ only needs the best path *into* each possible previous tag, not the full history. So you sweep left to right, keeping one running best-score per tag, and read the winner back at the end.

```python
import numpy as np
# Viterbi: best tag sequence under an HMM (log-space avoided for brevity)
def viterbi(obs, states, start_p, trans_p, emit_p):
    V = [{s: start_p[s] * emit_p[s][obs[0]] for s in states}]   # init column
    back = [{}]
    for t in range(1, len(obs)):
        V.append({}); back.append({})
        for s in states:
            # best previous state leading into s, times emission of obs[t]
            prev, score = max(
                ((p, V[t-1][p] * trans_p[p][s]) for p in states),
                key=lambda x: x[1])
            V[t][s] = score * emit_p[s][obs[t]]
            back[t][s] = prev
    # walk the backpointers from the best final state
    last = max(states, key=lambda s: V[-1][s])
    path = [last]
    for t in range(len(obs) - 1, 0, -1):
        last = back[t][last]; path.insert(0, last)
    return path

states = ["DET", "NOUN", "VERB"]
start  = {"DET": .8, "NOUN": .1, "VERB": .1}
trans  = {"DET":  {"DET":.1,"NOUN":.8,"VERB":.1},
          "NOUN": {"DET":.1,"NOUN":.2,"VERB":.7},
          "VERB": {"DET":.3,"NOUN":.4,"VERB":.3}}
emit   = {"DET":  {"the":.9,"dog":.05,"runs":.05},
          "NOUN": {"the":.1,"dog":.7,"runs":.2},
          "VERB": {"the":.05,"dog":.15,"runs":.8}}
print(viterbi(["the","dog","runs"], states, start, trans, emit))  # ['DET','NOUN','VERB']
```

```{mermaid}
graph LR
  T1["DET"] --> T2["NOUN"] --> T3["VERB"]
  T1 -.emits.-> W1["the"]
  T2 -.emits.-> W2["dog"]
  T3 -.emits.-> W3["runs"]
```

HMMs have a structural flaw: being generative, they model $P(\text{words}, \text{tags})$ and make strong independence assumptions, which makes it awkward to throw in rich, overlapping features (is the word capitalized? does it end in `-ing`? what's the previous word's suffix?). The **Conditional Random Field (CRF)** fixes this. It is **discriminative** — it models $P(\text{tags} \mid \text{words})$ directly — and scores an entire label sequence with a weighted sum of arbitrary feature functions, normalized over all possible sequences:

$$P(y \mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \exp\!\left(\sum_{i}\sum_{k} w_k\, f_k(y_{i-1}, y_i, x, i)\right)$$

where $f_k$ are feature functions, $w_k$ their learned weights, and $Z(x)$ a normalizer summing over all label sequences. Because features can look at any part of the input, CRFs consistently beat HMMs on NER and POS, and they remained the production default until neural sequence models — and the two even combine, as the CRF layer that still sits on top of many neural taggers to enforce valid label transitions.

## 28.5 — The core tasks of NLP

NLP is best understood as a family of recurring task shapes. Once you can map a problem onto one of these, you know roughly which models and metrics apply.

- **Text classification / sentiment analysis** — assign a label to a whole document: spam vs. not, or positive/negative/neutral sentiment. The workhorse classical recipe is TF-IDF features into logistic regression or a linear SVM.
- **Sequence labeling** — POS tagging and NER, as covered above: a label per token.
- **Parsing** — recover grammatical structure, either as a **constituency** tree (nested phrases) or a **dependency** tree (which word governs which). Useful when downstream logic needs to know *who did what to whom*.
- **Machine translation (MT)** — map a sentence in one language to another. This is the task that drove the **sequence-to-sequence** (encoder–decoder) architecture and later the attention mechanism.
- **Summarization** — compress a document. **Extractive** summarization selects salient sentences verbatim; **abstractive** generates new sentences, which is harder but more fluent.
- **Question answering (QA)** — answer a question, either by extracting a span from a given passage (extractive QA) or generating an answer from knowledge.
- **Topic modeling** — an *unsupervised* task: discover latent themes across a corpus with no labels (next section).

The reason this taxonomy matters is that it cuts across the classical/neural divide. A transformer and a TF-IDF-plus-SVM solve the *same* classification task — they differ only in the representation feeding the final decision. Learning the task shapes first means new architectures slot into a structure you already understand.

## 28.6 — Topic modeling with LDA, and the transformer turn

Sometimes you have a mountain of documents and no labels, and you simply want to know what they are *about*. **Topic modeling** discovers recurring themes automatically. The dominant classical method is **Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA)**, a probabilistic generative model with a clean story: every document is a *mixture* of a few topics, and every topic is a *distribution* over words. A "sports" topic puts high probability on `game`, `team`, `score`; a news article might be 70% politics and 30% economics. LDA reverses this generative story — given the documents, it infers the hidden topic-word and document-topic distributions that most plausibly produced them. The output is interpretable clusters of words you read off as themes, with no supervision required.

Then came the shift that reorganized the entire field. For decades NLP meant hand-built features, task-specific models (an HMM here, an LDA there, a CRF for tagging), and word vectors that ignored context. The **transformer** (2017) and the **pretraining** paradigm collapsed this into one recipe: pretrain a single large model on oceans of raw text with a self-supervised objective, then adapt it to any task. **BERT** popularized **contextual embeddings** — the vector for `bank` now *depends on the sentence*, so the money and river senses finally separate. Because the transformer is the subject of its own chapters, the takeaway here is narrow: nearly every task in 28.5, once solved with a bespoke model, is now solved by fine-tuning or prompting a pretrained transformer, usually at much higher accuracy.

```{mermaid}
graph LR
  A["Classical era<br/>BoW · TF-IDF · HMM · CRF · LDA<br/>one model per task"] --> B["Embedding era<br/>word2vec · GloVe<br/>static dense vectors"]
  B --> C["Transformer era<br/>pretrain once · adapt to all tasks<br/>contextual embeddings"]
```

::: {.callout-warning}
"Classical NLP is obsolete" is a costly misconception. TF-IDF plus logistic regression trains in seconds, needs no GPU, is fully interpretable, and is a strong baseline that frequently beats a hastily fine-tuned transformer on small, narrow datasets. Always build the cheap baseline first.
:::

## 28.7 — Evaluating language systems

How do you grade a system whose output is text? Metrics in NLP fall into two camps: classification-style scores for labeling tasks, and overlap-style scores for generation.

For classification, sequence labeling, and extractive QA, the standard is **precision, recall, and F1**. Precision asks "of the things I labeled positive, how many were right?"; recall asks "of the true positives, how many did I find?"; F1 is their harmonic mean, balancing the two:

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{precision} \cdot \text{recall}}{\text{precision} + \text{recall}}$$

For NER, F1 is computed over entity spans — a prediction counts only if both the span boundaries and the type are exactly right, which makes it a demanding metric.

For generation tasks the trouble is that there is no single correct output — many translations or summaries are equally valid — so we measure *overlap* with one or more human references. **BLEU** (for translation) checks what fraction of the candidate's n-grams appear in the reference, combining several n-gram lengths and penalizing outputs that are too short. **ROUGE** (for summarization) is recall-oriented, measuring how much of the *reference's* content the candidate covers.

| Metric | Task | Measures | Orientation |
|--------|------|----------|-------------|
| F1 | Classification, NER, QA | Correct labels/spans | Balanced |
| BLEU | Machine translation | N-gram overlap with reference | Precision-leaning |
| ROUGE | Summarization | Overlap with reference content | Recall-leaning |

```python
import numpy as np
def f1(tp, fp, fn):
    prec = tp / (tp + fp) if tp + fp else 0.0
    rec  = tp / (tp + fn) if tp + fn else 0.0
    return 2 * prec * rec / (prec + rec) if prec + rec else 0.0

print(round(f1(tp=40, fp=10, fn=20), 3))  # 0.727
```

::: {.callout-warning}
BLEU and ROUGE only measure surface word overlap. A perfect paraphrase using synonyms scores low, while a fluent but factually wrong summary can score high. They correlate weakly with human judgment, which is why modern evaluation increasingly leans on embedding-based scores (BERTScore) and human or LLM-based judging.
:::

## 28.8 — Key takeaways

- NLP's central problem is **representation**: every advance — counts, embeddings, contextual vectors — is a better way to turn text into numbers.
- **Preprocessing** (tokenization, stemming/lemmatization, stop-words) matters for classical models but is largely skipped in transformer pipelines; subword tokenization (BPE, WordPiece) is the modern default.
- **BoW and TF-IDF** are sparse and ignore meaning; **word embeddings** (word2vec, GloVe) are dense and encode similarity, but assign one vector per word regardless of context.
- **HMMs and CRFs** were the sequence-labeling workhorses for POS and NER; HMMs decode with Viterbi, while CRFs win by being discriminative and feature-rich, and still appear as a layer atop neural taggers.
- Learn the **task shapes** — classification, sequence labeling, parsing, translation, summarization, QA, topic modeling — because architectures change but the tasks endure.
- **Transformers** unified the field: pretrain once, adapt everywhere, with contextual embeddings — but cheap classical baselines (TF-IDF + logistic regression) are still worth building first.
- Evaluate with **F1** for labeling, **BLEU** for translation, **ROUGE** for summarization — and remember all three measure surface overlap, not meaning.

## 28.9 — See also

- **Large Language Models** — the modern endpoint of this chapter's arc, where pretrained transformers scale to general-purpose language ability.
- **Attention & Transformers** — the architecture that produced contextual embeddings and replaced the classical toolkit.
- **Recurrent & Sequence Models** — the seq2seq and RNN models that bridged CRFs and transformers.
- **Linear & Logistic Models** and **Support Vector Machines & Kernels** — the classifiers behind TF-IDF text classification.
- **Probabilistic Graphical Models** — the formal home of HMMs and CRFs.
- **Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning** — related unsupervised structure-finding, alongside LDA topic modeling.
- **Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search** — where dense embeddings from this chapter power modern semantic retrieval.
- **Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment** — how the pretrained models that now dominate NLP are adapted to specific tasks.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 27 · 👁️ Computer Vision](ch27.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [29 · 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing →](ch29.html)
